title: Claude Mythos explained..
summary: Claude Mythos represents a major intelligence jump similar to o1's leap. The video discusses the model's performance benchmarks, inference capabilities, and pricing. The author raises concerns about token access being restricted to certain companies, creating disparity as AI intelligence advances.
writeup: Kilobright's Code examines the release of Claude Mythos, Anthropic's most capable model, which scores 77% on SWE-bench Pro versus Opus at 53%, a performance leap comparable to OpenAI's o1 jump in September 2024. Rather than a broad release, Anthropic initially restricted access to a handful of investor-partners (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Nvidia, Cisco, JPMorgan), framing the rollout around cybersecurity risk. The central argument is that the real story is not the cybersecurity framing but the structural pattern it reveals: each step-change in model intelligence arrives faster than adoption can follow, concentrating the advantage among those who can pay (priced at $125 per million output tokens) or who hold privatized access.
key_points: Claude Mythos scores 77% on SWE-bench Pro versus Opus at 53%, and shows a similarly large gap on terminal bench, marking a performance step-change Anthropic compares to the o1 release in September 2024.

Anthropic restricted initial access to investor-partners with equity stakes (Microsoft Series C and G, Google Series C and E, Amazon Series D and E, Nvidia Series G, Cisco Series E, JPMorgan convertible loan), creating what the video calls a privatization of tokens where higher-level intelligence is rationed by financial relationship rather than open availability.

At a planned price of $125 per million output tokens, Claude Mythos sits near the top of the market alongside OpenAI GPT-5 Pro at $180, making it economically inaccessible for broad use cases and tying access to return-on-investment calculations.

Anthropic does not own its full inference stack: training and inference still run on Amazon, Google, and Microsoft hyperscalers, and the model will be available through Claude API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex, and Microsoft Foundry, limiting Anthropic's control over token generation despite its $50 billion data-center commitment.

The performance gap created by Mythos will likely close faster than it appears: DeepSeek R1 matched o1's capabilities within five months of o1's release in January 2025, suggesting a similar compression window is plausible here.

The author argues the deeper issue is AI adoption velocity: companies that allocate the right tier of intelligence to the right problem class gain compounding advantage, while the gap between AI-adopting and non-adopting organizations widens with each capability jump.
